content.text: User Feedback Collection for Claude Products

User feedback is the primary signal for evaluating whether a Claude-integrated product is producing outputs that meet user needs. Unlike infrastructure metrics (error rates, latency, token counts), feedback captures the subjective quality dimension: was the response helpful, accurate, appropriately detailed, formatted correctly for the context? Designing a robust feedback collection system requires choosing the right collection channels for the product context, structuring feedback data to enable analysis, and routing feedback to the teams that can act on it. Doing any one of these without the others produces either data that cannot be used or processes that do not actually improve the product.

attrs.alt: Five user feedback collection channels: thumb rating, regenerate clicks, free text comment, support tickets, and session abandonment — all flowing into a unified feedback pipeline

content.text: Feedback Signal Types and Their Trade-offs

Different feedback channels produce different signal types with different volume, noise, and actionability characteristics. A thumb rating (positive/negative) produces high-volume, low-noise binary signal — useful for tracking aggregate quality trends but insufficient for diagnosing specific issues. Free-text comment produces low-volume, high-signal qualitative data — useful for understanding specific failure modes but not for measuring overall quality at scale. Most production Claude products need at least two channels to cover both dimensions.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum
from datetime import datetime

class FeedbackChannel(str, Enum):
    THUMB_RATING      = "thumb_rating"     # +1 / -1
    REGENERATE_CLICK  = "regenerate_click" # implicit: user wanted different output
    FREE_TEXT         = "free_text"        # user typed a comment
    SUPPORT_TICKET    = "support_ticket"   # formal support request
    ABANDONMENT       = "abandonment"      # session ended without task completion

class FeedbackSentiment(str, Enum):
    POSITIVE  = "positive"
    NEGATIVE  = "negative"
    NEUTRAL   = "neutral"
    UNKNOWN   = "unknown"   # implicit signals before classification

@dataclass
class FeedbackRecord:
    feedback_id: str
    session_id: str
    user_id: str | None         # None if anonymous
    channel: FeedbackChannel
    sentiment: FeedbackSentiment
    message_index: int          # which message in the conversation was rated
    response_text_hash: str     # SHA of the response; for deduplication
    free_text_comment: str | None
    model_id: str
    collected_at: datetime
    metadata: dict = field(default_factory=dict)

    def is_actionable(self) -> bool:
        """
        Returns True for feedback that can drive a specific improvement action.
        Implicit signals and simple thumbs-down without comment are less actionable.
        """
        if self.channel == FeedbackChannel.FREE_TEXT:
            return bool(self.free_text_comment and len(self.free_text_comment) > 20)
        if self.channel == FeedbackChannel.SUPPORT_TICKET:
            return True
        if self.channel == FeedbackChannel.THUMB_RATING and self.sentiment == FeedbackSentiment.NEGATIVE:
            return bool(self.free_text_comment)
        return False

    def requires_human_review(self) -> bool:
        if self.channel == FeedbackChannel.SUPPORT_TICKET:
            return True
        if self.free_text_comment and any(
            kw in self.free_text_comment.lower()
            for kw in ["wrong", "harmful", "offensive", "incorrect", "dangerous"]
        ):
            return True
        return False

The requires_human_review method implements keyword-based routing for safety-relevant feedback. A free-text comment containing "wrong" or "harmful" is routed to human review regardless of sentiment classification — automated classifiers may label a report of harmful output as "negative sentiment" and route it to the same queue as a complaint about verbose formatting, missing the safety-critical nature of the feedback. Explicit keyword routing for safety vocabulary ensures these reports are handled separately.
Structuring the Feedback Pipeline

A feedback pipeline that collects raw signals without processing them is not useful — it is a data sink that accumulates without producing insights. The pipeline transforms raw feedback records into classified, scored, deduplicated signals that can drive product decisions. Each stage of the pipeline reduces noise and increases actionability.

The metrics-only routing queue receives all implicit negative feedback (thumb-down without comment, regenerate clicks) that cannot be attributed to a specific issue. These records contribute to aggregate trend metrics — the negative feedback rate over time — but are not routed to engineering for action because there is no actionable information in them. Routing them to engineering would create noise in the action queue without improving quality. The quality/format queues receive feedback with enough specificity to drive a concrete action.
Aggregate Metrics and Trend Tracking

Individual feedback records are action drivers; aggregate feedback metrics are health indicators. The aggregate metrics track how overall response quality changes over time — after a model version upgrade, after a system prompt change, or after a new feature release. A sudden drop in the positive feedback rate after a deployment is an early warning that something changed in a way that users notice, even before support tickets or error metrics reflect the issue.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import date
from typing import Sequence

@dataclass
class FeedbackAggregateWindow:
    window_date: date
    total_feedback: int
    positive_count: int
    negative_count: int
    neutral_count: int
    safety_escalations: int
    by_category: dict[str, int] = field(default_factory=dict)

    def positive_rate(self) -> float:
        rated = self.positive_count + self.negative_count
        return self.positive_count / rated if rated > 0 else 0.0

    def safety_rate(self) -> float:
        return self.safety_escalations / self.total_feedback if self.total_feedback > 0 else 0.0

    def quality_signal(self) -> str:
        pr = self.positive_rate()
        if pr >= 0.85:
            return "GOOD"
        if pr >= 0.70:
            return "ACCEPTABLE"
        if pr >= 0.55:
            return "DEGRADED"
        return "CRITICAL"

def detect_regression(
    windows: Sequence[FeedbackAggregateWindow],
    baseline_windows: int = 7,
    threshold_drop: float = 0.10,
) -> bool:
    """
    Returns True if the most recent window's positive_rate has dropped
    more than `threshold_drop` below the average of the baseline windows.
    """
    if len(windows) < baseline_windows + 1:
        return False
    baseline = windows[:baseline_windows]
    baseline_avg = sum(w.positive_rate() for w in baseline) / baseline_windows
    current = windows[-1].positive_rate()
    return (baseline_avg - current) > threshold_drop

The detect_regression function implements a simple baseline comparison: if the current window's positive rate is more than 10 percentage points below the 7-day baseline average, a regression is flagged. This is intentionally simple — more sophisticated anomaly detection requires more data and more engineering effort. For most Claude products, a 10-point drop in positive feedback rate is already a signal worth investigating, and the simple comparison catches it without requiring an ML anomaly detection system. The threshold_drop parameter allows calibration per product.
Closing the Feedback Loop with Users

Collecting feedback is only half of the feedback loop. The other half is communicating back to users that their feedback was received and acted on. Without this, feedback rates decline over time: users stop providing feedback when they observe no change in response to their reports. Closing the loop does not require responding personally to every piece of feedback — it requires making visible the product changes that were driven by user feedback, so that users who experienced the issue can see that it was addressed.
Changelog entries that reference feedback:

When a product release includes an improvement driven by user feedback, the changelog entry should say so explicitly: "Improved response formatting in code explanations based on user feedback." Users who submitted formatting complaints recognise the reference and understand that feedback drives product decisions.
Direct acknowledgement for support ticket reporters:

Users who submitted formal support tickets should receive a notification when the issue they reported is resolved. The notification does not need to be elaborate — a brief message connecting the fix to their report is sufficient. This closes the loop for the highest-investment form of feedback.
In-product feedback transparency:

Some products show a "what changed recently" panel or a "we heard you" banner when users first encounter an improved version. This approach closes the loop at scale without requiring personalised communication for every feedback reporter.
Public feedback summaries for enterprise accounts:

Enterprise customers with support agreements expect a regular summary of what feedback was collected from their users, what was escalated to Anthropic, and what product improvements resulted. This is the highest-effort form of loop closure but maintains the highest level of customer trust.
Designing Effective In-Product Feedback UIs

The design of the feedback UI determines the volume, quality, and coverage of feedback collected. A poorly designed UI collects feedback from outliers (users who are extremely satisfied or extremely frustrated) but misses the middle — the majority who had a mediocre experience and will not submit feedback unless it is effortless. The goal is to make feedback submission the path of least resistance when a user encounters a response that is not what they needed.
UI Element
Placement
Expected Volume
Signal Quality
Thumb up/down icon pair
Below every response, always visible
5–15% of responses rated
Low — binary only
Optional comment field after thumb-down
Appears on negative rating click
10–25% of negative raters add comment
High — specific and voluntary
Category picker after thumb-down
Appears on negative rating click
30–60% of negative raters select category
Medium — structured but limited options
Copy/edit action tracking
Instrument clipboard and editor events
High — nearly all users who accepted output
Low — only signals acceptance, not satisfaction
Periodic satisfaction survey
In-session after N interactions
5–10% response rate
High — captures session-level experience

The category picker after a thumb-down is the highest-impact single UX investment for feedback quality. It converts a binary "this was bad" signal into a structured signal by asking the user to choose from 4–5 categories (Wrong answer, Unhelpful, Too long/short, Offensive, Other). The structured category is immediately usable for routing and aggregation, unlike free text which requires classification before it can be acted on. Design the category list based on the feedback you actually expect to receive — generic categories like "Quality issue" are not useful; specific categories like "Factual error" and "Misunderstood my question" are.
Privacy Considerations in Feedback Data Collection

Feedback records contain three categories of potentially sensitive data: the user's identity (user_id), the content of the response that was rated (response text), and the content of the free-text comment (which may contain personal information, confidential content, or the topic the user was discussing). Privacy-by-design for feedback systems minimises the collection of each category to what is necessary for the intended use.

from dataclasses import dataclass
import hashlib

@dataclass
class PrivacyPreservingFeedbackRecord:
    """
    Version of FeedbackRecord with PII minimisation applied.
    """
    feedback_id: str
    session_id_hash: str          # SHA-256 of session ID; not reversible
    user_cohort: str              # bucketed attribute; never raw user ID
    channel: str
    sentiment: str
    model_id: str
    response_length_bucket: str   # "short" | "medium" | "long"; not raw text
    free_text_comment: str | None # user-authored; may contain PII

    @classmethod
    def from_raw(
        cls,
        raw: "FeedbackRecord",
        cohort: str,
    ) -> "PrivacyPreservingFeedbackRecord":
        session_hash = hashlib.sha256(raw.session_id.encode()).hexdigest()[:16]
        resp_len = len(raw.metadata.get("response_text", ""))
        if resp_len < 200:
            bucket = "short"
        elif resp_len < 1000:
            bucket = "medium"
        else:
            bucket = "long"
        return cls(
            feedback_id=raw.feedback_id,
            session_id_hash=session_hash,
            user_cohort=cohort,
            channel=raw.channel.value,
            sentiment=raw.sentiment.value,
            model_id=raw.model_id,
            response_length_bucket=bucket,
            free_text_comment=raw.free_text_comment,
        )

    def requires_pii_scan(self) -> bool:
        """
        Free-text comments may contain PII. Flag for scanning before
        storing in analytics tables accessible to non-privacy-cleared systems.
        """
        return self.free_text_comment is not None and len(self.free_text_comment) > 0

The from_raw class method applies privacy transformations at the point of ingest, not downstream. Hashing the session ID prevents cross-session linkage without losing the ability to deduplicate feedback from the same session. Using a cohort bucket instead of a raw user ID allows segment analysis (new users vs. returning users, enterprise vs. consumer) without creating a user-identified feedback dataset. The requires_pii_scan flag ensures that free-text comments — which may contain names, phone numbers, or other personal information shared as part of the prompt context — are scanned for PII before being ingested into analytics pipelines that do not have appropriate access controls.
Summary

User feedback collection for Claude products requires a multi-channel strategy: explicit signals (thumb ratings, free-text comments) for qualitative insight, implicit signals (regenerate clicks, abandonment) for volume-scale trend tracking, and support ticket routing for actionable escalations. A structured pipeline classifies raw feedback into actionable categories, routes safety-relevant feedback to human review, and feeds aggregate metrics into daily quality dashboards. The detect_regression function provides an automated early warning for quality drops that follow product changes, making feedback a continuous feedback loop rather than a post-hoc review process.
